description:
You are a backup-operator who must prove a small static web site can be restored from a
    supplied archive and served locally.

    1. A compressed backup archive already exists at
       /home/user/backup/site_backup.tar.gz .
    2. Restore the archive into a **new** directory named
       /home/user/restored_site (create the directory if necessary).
    3. Start a simple web-server that serves the restored_site directory on TCP
       port 8000 so that a browser visiting
       http://localhost:8000/index.html receives the restored file.
       • Any commonly-installed, non-root web-server tool is acceptable
         (e.g. “python3 -m http.server”, “busybox httpd”, etc.).
       • The server must keep running after your commands finish (run it in the
         background or by using a terminal multiplexer).
    4. Create a plain-text log file at /home/user/restoration.log containing
       exactly two lines, in this order and with no surrounding whitespace:
           EXTRACT_OK
           SERVER_OK
       The automated tests will parse this file verbatim, so do not add extra
       spaces, blank lines or timestamps.

    Final system-state that the grader will check:
       • Directory /home/user/restored_site exists.
       • File   /home/user/restored_site/index.html exists and is identical to
         what was inside the supplied archive.
       • A process is listening on TCP port 8000 and serves the above file; a
         request like
              curl -s http://localhost:8000/index.html
         must return the exact content restored from the archive.
       • File /home/user/restoration.log exists and its two lines are exactly
         “EXTRACT_OK” followed by “SERVER_OK”.

    Hint: Only 2-3 terminal commands are needed: unpack, launch server, write
    log. Make sure you run them from any directory you prefer, but keep all
    paths absolute so the grader can validate the outcome.
truth:
Pre-existing artefacts placed before the agent runs: 1. Directory: /home/user/backup 2. File: /home/user/backup/site_backup.tar.gz – This tarball has exactly one entry named “index.html”. – The internal path inside the archive is: index.html – The uncompressed index.html contents are exactly (including newlines):

Backup Restore Successful

Verification details the test harness will use: • After the agent finishes, it will untar /home/user/backup/site_backup.tar.gz into a temporary directory and byte-compare the resulting index.html with /home/user/restored_site/index.html . They must be identical. • It will read /home/user/restoration.log and expect **exactly**: Line-1: “EXTRACT_OK” Line-2: “SERVER_OK” • It will run: curl -s http://localhost:8000/index.html and verify the response body equals the content shown above. • It will check that a process owned by the user is listening on TCP port 8000 (e.g. by examining the output of “ss -ltnp” or “lsof -i :8000”) and that the command line of that process contains the word “http.server” (for python) or “httpd”/“busybox” depending on the tool used. File/Directory permissions: • /home/user/backup and /home/user/backup/site_backup.tar.gz are readable by the current user. • The agent has write permission in /home/user and all sub-directories.
